Learning Python from a JavaScript Angle (31): So What Do You Want to Learn Next?
JavaScript
Learn Python From a JavaScript Angle
13th iThome Ironman Contest

I finally crossed the finish line of the Ironman contest. So for day 31, the last one, I'm just going to sit down and chat with you about how the whole thing went — what I learned about Python coming from JavaScript, and why I think the real takeaway is to keep learning no matter what tool you pick up.
/en/python/2021/10/01/learning-python-from-javascript-30-web-scraping/
Learning Python From JavaScript (30): Building a Web Scraper
Learning Python From JavaScript
iThome Ironman Contest (13th)

We've picked up a fair amount of Python by now, and this is the last day of the Ironman contest — so let's put it all together and build a simple scraper for the iThome Ironman site. Using Requests plus BeautifulSoup, we'll grab the topic list, page through each topic's contestants, and save everything out to JSON.
/en/python/2021/09/30/python-29-beautifulsoup/
Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle (29) - BeautifulSoup
Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle

We're heading into the home stretch of the Ironman contest, so this post covers a really common package: BeautifulSoup. It's one of the go-to tools for web scraping in Python, used to parse the HTML you download so you can pull out exactly the bits you want. Let's learn how to use it.
/en/python/2021/09/29/python-from-js-28-closures/
Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle (28) - Closures

JavaScript has this thing called a closure. Does Python have it too? In this chapter we'll have a relaxed chat about how to write a closure in Python if you want one — including the one syntax trap that'll throw a SyntaxError if you try to copy JavaScript too literally.
/en/python/2021/09/28/learning-python-from-javascript-27-pass-by-value-reference/
Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle (27): Pass by Value or Pass by Reference?
第 13 屆 iT邦幫忙鐵人賽

We're almost at the end of the iThome Ironman series, and I just realized I skipped something kind of important — pass by value vs pass by reference. So this post is a quick chat about how values and references work in Python, and how they line up with what you already know from JavaScript.
/en/python/2021/09/27/learning-python-from-javascript-26-shebang/
Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle (26) - What That #! Line at the Top Actually Means

We've covered a lot of heavy stuff already, so let's take it easy this time and talk about something simple. You've probably seen that weird #!/usr/bin/env python line at the top of Python files. What is it? It's called a shebang, and here's what it does and why you'd want it.
/en/python/2021/09/26/python-25-exception-handling/
Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle (25) - Exception Handling

Next up is something really, really important: handling errors, which we usually call exception handling. We'll look at try/except in Python, how to target specific error types, what finally is for, and Python's raise (the equivalent of JavaScript's throw), all compared side by side with JavaScript.
/en/python/2021/09/25/python-from-js-perspective-24-inheritance/
Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle (24) - Inheritance

Last time I teased that inheritance was coming up next, so of course this post is all about inheritance. We start with a quick JavaScript refresher (both the prototype version and the ES6 class version), then jump into how Python does it — spoiler: there's no extends keyword, and super works a lot like the one you already know.
/en/python/2021/09/24/python-from-js-23-class/
Learning Python From a JS Angle (23): Classes — and Why JavaScript Isn't Quite OOP

A quick, low-key chat about Python's class syntax. Along the way we untangle a question that bugged me for ages: is JavaScript actually object-oriented? Spoiler — kind of, but not the way you think. Then we build a Python class step by step, meet __init__ and self, and see how close it all feels to JS.
/en/python/2021/09/23/learning-python-from-javascript-22-github-api/
Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle (22): Hitting the GitHub API

We've picked up a fair bit of Python by now, so it's about time we actually built something with it — that's how the concepts really stick. In this one we'll wire up the GitHub API with the requests package, let the user type in any GitHub username, pull back the repos that account has starred, and dump the result into a JSON file.
